title: T2_15N_HSQC.nan
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"50qeo","text":"This protocol describes running 15N T2 HSQC experiment with CPMG spin-lock, temperature compensation, sensitivity enhancement, gradient coherence selection, water flip-back, and optional 13C decoupling. This is a pseudo-3D experiment, with the third dimension F1(T2) sampling T2 relaxation delays, which are defined by a loop counter list provided in a separate text file. Temperature compensation involves application of pulses prior to recycle delay delivering the same total RF power for each T2 delay.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":32,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":187,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":497,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":37,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":277,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":497,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5njbr","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2tj73","text":"This experiment is primarily used for studying protein backbone dynamics on the picosecond to nanosecond scale in combination with 15N T1 HSQC, {1H}-15N HNOE, etc.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":131,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":145,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":149,"length":2},{"style":"sub","offset":136,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ceh2h","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"8ckab","text":"Required isotope labeling: U-15N, or U-15N,13C (with or without 2H). Also suitable for samples with selective (\"sparse\") 15N-labeling of certain amino acid types.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":29,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":39,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":43,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":64,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":121,"length":2}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"audpj","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"80ecl","text":"Optimal MW is ≤ 30 kDa. For larger systems 15N T2 TROSY may be more appropriate.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":43,"length":2},{"style":"sub","offset":48,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7h7bm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"434es","text":"This protocol uses the Topspin library pulseprograms hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d (long CPMG cycle) or hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d.2 (short CPMG cycle). ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":53,"length":18},{"style":"italic","offset":93,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"2vhd5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"ff12o","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"6vbjc","text":"The number of directly acquired points (2 TD) should be set so the acquisition time t3,max (3 AQ) is between ~50 ms (for larger proteins ~25 kDa) and ~120 ms (for smaller proteins). Longer times may cause excessive probe and sample heating during 15N decoupling, and resolve undesirable 3JHN,HA splittings.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":40,"length":4},{"style":"bold","offset":92,"length":4},{"style":"sub","offset":85,"length":5},{"style":"sub","offset":289,"length":5},{"style":"sup","offset":247,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":287,"length":1}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bq7a7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"3gsh7","text":"\"Effective\" 1JNH coupling value CNST4 determines the length of the INEPT transfer delays. For larger proteins CNST4 can be increased (i.e. \u003e 92 Hz) to reduce losses due to relaxation. CNST4 can be optimized by arraying using popt with 15N HSQC experiment in 1D mode.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":12,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":235,"length":2},{"style":"sub","offset":14,"length":2},{"style":"bold","offset":32,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":110,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":184,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":225,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9tk0r","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bqrgp","text":"For samples with 13C labeling use -DLABEL_CN ZGOPTNS flag to enable 13C decoupling during 15N evolution. 13C channel offset O2P should be set ~110 ppm (middle of 13C aliphatic and 13CO shift range).","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":17,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":68,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":90,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":105,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":162,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":180,"length":2},{"style":"bold","offset":34,"length":11},{"style":"bold","offset":124,"length":3}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"283ks","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"9r27e","text":"Since 15N T2 HSQC is a quantitative experiment, and the CPMG spin-lock generates significant probe heating, it is recommended to use longer D1 recycle delays (\u003e 2s) and a sufficient number of dummy scans DS. 1H spectral width 3 SW should be set a few ppm wider than required by the peak dispersion to allow for more \"empty\" noise regions for better baseline correction during FT processing.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sup","offset":6,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":208,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":11,"length":1},{"style":"bold","offset":140,"length":2},{"style":"bold","offset":204,"length":2},{"style":"bold","offset":226,"length":4}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4pvr7","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"96e9a","text":"Exact values of T2 relaxation delays are equal to loop counter value (from VCLIST) times CPMG loop length. The latter value is stored as D31 delay, computed internally by the pulseprogram and cannot be set directly by the user. The exact value of CPMG loop length D31 depends on the P30 CPMG 180º 15N pulse length, and should be approximately 16 ms or 8 ms for long CMPG loop (hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d) and short CMPG loop (hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d.2) pulse sequences, respectively. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":17,"length":1},{"style":"bold","offset":75,"length":6},{"style":"bold","offset":137,"length":3},{"style":"bold","offset":264,"length":3},{"style":"bold","offset":283,"length":3},{"style":"sup","offset":297,"length":2},{"style":"italic","offset":377,"length":18},{"style":"italic","offset":418,"length":20}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"65ce5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4c2d1","text":"For optimal fitting precision, use at least ~8 T2 points with the longest delay should be at least as long at the expected T2 relaxation time. This may vary from protein to protein, as T2 decreases with MW. For safe operating limits regrading P30 and PLW23/PLdB23 power levels check the Bruker \"Typical Pulses\" manual for the particular probe installed with your instrument, or consult your NMR facility manager.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"sub","offset":48,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":124,"length":1},{"style":"sub","offset":186,"length":1},{"style":"bold","offset":243,"length":3},{"style":"bold","offset":251,"length":5},{"style":"bold","offset":257,"length":6}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
before_start: {"blocks":[{"key":"3vmkh","text":"A sample must be inserted in the magnet either locally by the user after training, or by facility staff if running remotely.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"6d9li","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5dk42","text":"This protocol requires a sample is locked, tuned/matched on 1H, 13C and 15N channels, and shimmed. At a minimum, 1H 90° pulse width and offset O1 should be calibrated and a 1D proton spectrum with water suppression has been collected according to the protocol PRESAT_bio.nan. Prior acquisition of a 2D 15N HSQC is also recommended, according to protocol HSQC_15N.nan.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":260,"length":14},{"style":"bold","offset":299,"length":11},{"style":"bold","offset":354,"length":13}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b0ee5","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cauaj","text":"General aspects of the use of the bioTop module are described in more detail in the protocols listed below.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"bold","offset":33,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"af3n1","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"au92p","text":"It is recommended to calibrate 1H carrier offset, 1H H2O selective flip-back pulse, as well as 1H, 13C, and 15N 90° pulse widths using the \"Optimization\" tab of BioTop. Alternatively, 1H 90° pulse width and offset can be calibrated using other methods, such as pulsecal or calibo1p1. Additional parameters, like 15N and 13C offsets and spectral width can be either optimized or manually entered in the \"Optimization\" tab of BioTop. Note that since BioTop optimizations are saved in the dataset folder, all experiments should be created under the same dataset name when using BioTop for acquisition setup.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"anhdc","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"auhhr","text":"Refer to protocols ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"51f75","text":" Acquisition Setup Workflow, Solution NMR Structural Biology","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"7ho3e","text":"PRESAT_bio.nan","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4gbba","text":"HSQC_15N.nan","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5q2hl","text":"HNCO_3D.nan","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"4ltiv","text":"Biotop-Calibration and Acquisition setup","type":"ordered-list-item","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"d8bqg","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: Start with existing dataset containing 1D 1H or 15N HSQC data for this sample.
Open create dataset window with edc command or through menu. Edit text in Title box.
When Lock, Tune, Shim are complete proceed to the specific protocol for your desired pulseprogram(s).

To select starting parameter set, check 'Read parameterset' box, and click Select. For standard NAN parameter sets, change the Source directory at upper right corner of the window: Source = /opt/NAN_SB/par Click 'Select' to bring up list of parameter sets. Select T2_15N_HSQC_SHORT_xxx.par or T2_15N_HSQC_LONG_xxx.par , where xxx=900,800 or 600. Click OK at bottom of window to create the new EXPNO directory.

Loading the T2_15N_HSQC_short/long_xxx.par parameter set enters the default parameters into the experiment directory. While they represent a good starting point, they may not be fully optimal or accurate for your particular sample or spectrometer hardware. The probe- and solvent-specific parameters, specifically the 1H 90° pulse length, and possibly the 13C and 15N 90° pulse lengths, along with other dependent pulse widths and powers may need to be updated. There are two ways of automatically updating experimental parameters: 1) Use getprosol command, which typically only updates proton pulse widths and power levels. It is most useful for running routine experiments using the default parameters. 2) Or use bioTop module that organizes calibrated and defined parameters for a dataset.

Type 'expt' to calculate the expected run time go to step #2.3 Type ' rga' or click on 'Gain' in Topspin Acquire menu to execute automatic gain adjustment. Type ' zg ' or click on 'Run' in Topspin Acquire menu to begin acquisition.

You can always check the first FID by typing efp to execute an exponential multiplied Fourier transform. It will ask for a FID #, choose the default #1. You can evaluate the 1D spectrum for amide proton signal to noise and water suppression.

Loading experimental parameters from BioTop : If you previously performed parameter calibrations using the "Optimizations" tab of the BioTop GUI, or entered parameters manually in the "Optimizations" tab, you can type btprep at the command line. See protocol 'BioTop: calibration and acquisition setup' and attached Bruker manual 'biotop.pdf' for details.
If not previously done, tune Nitrogen and Carbon channels. Return to the 'Acquire' menu and click 'Tune' (or type atma or atmm on command line). Re-shim after tuning.

Loading pulse widths and power levels with getprosol : Use the calibrated proton P1 value obtained from the proton experiment ( protocol PRESAT_bio.nan) and note the standard power level attenuation in dB for P1 (PLW1); otherwise type calibo1p1 and wait till finished. Then execute the getprosol command: getprosol 1H [ calibrated P1 value] [power level for P1] e.g. getprosol 1H 9.9 -13.14. Where for example, the calibrated P1=9.9 at power level -13.14 dB attenuation This also loads 15 N and 13 C pulse widths and power levels from the PROSOL table, and are assumed to be sufficiently accurate. go to step #2.3

Then examine the parameters in Acqpars 'ased' mode (click 'pulse' icon), or type ased . Most of the default parameters should be appropriate, however it's useful to compare values in the fields against those proposed by the original parameter file and the pulseprogram comments. CNST4 - one-bond 1 J NH coupling effective value (≥93 Hz); used to calculate INEPT transfer delays. CNST11 - multiplicity factor; 4 - optimized for NH (default), or 8 - optimized for NH/NH 2 CNST12 - multiplicity factor; 4 - optimized for NH (default), or 8 - optimized for NH/NH 2 D1 - recyle delay (≥2s for protonated samples, ≥ 3s for perdeuterated samples) D31 - length of single CPMG loop (~16 ms for hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d , or ~8 ms hsqct2etf3gpsitc3d.2 ); computed internally by the pulse program and cannot be changed; used to calculate exact values of T 2 delays VCLIST: Loop counter file for T 2 delays. Click on dots button ' ... ' to select, or on ' E ' to edit. Length of each CMPG loop is given by D31 ; use at least ~8 points. The longest T 2 dealy should not exceed 250 ms. ZGOPTNS flags -DLABEL_CN - enable 13 C decoupling during 15 N evolution for 13 C, 15 N-labeled samples (default) channel f1: P1 - 1 H 90º high power pulse (calibrated with calibo1p1 or BioTop) SPdB1 - power level [dB] for 1 H H 2 O flip-back shaped pulse (calibrated with BioTop) Channel f2 (only for ZGOPTNS -DLABEL_CN ): CNST21 - 13CO offset (~173 ppm, calibrated with BioTop) for 13 C decoupling CNST22 - 13CA offset (~53 ppm, calibrated with BioTop) for 13 C decoupling channel f3: P21 - 15 N 90º high power pulse (calibrated with BioTop) at PLW3 / PLdB3 P30 - 15 N 180º pulse for CPMG spin-lock at PLW23 / PLdB23, reduced power (defined in PROSOL). Should not be shorter than 80 us on most probes (90 us on 1.7 mm probes)! PLW23 / PLdB23 - power level for P30 15 N 180º pulse for CPMG spin-lock (calculated automatically with getprosol or btprep ). Should not exceed PLW3 / PLdB3 power! 15 N CPMG spin lock is the main source of probe/sample heating. Excessive heating may lead to poor quality spectra and in extreme cases cause probe damage. For safe operating limits regrading P30 and PLW23 / PLdB23 check the Bruker "Typical Pulses" manual for the particular probe installed with your instrument, or consult your NMR facility manager. To minimize sample/probe heating set the longest T 2 delay (largest VCLIST counter) as short as possible while allowing sufficient precision of T 2 decay fit. Use a large number of dummy scans ( DS ≥128) for temperature equilibration. Enable autoshim to compensate for shim drift due to probe heating. If probe heating is still an issue, you can also set longer P30 pulse length (~100 us) than configured in PROSOL table. It should not be excessively long, or the refocusing performance would be reduced, especially at high magnetic fields. Recalculate PLW23 power in watts according to PLW23 = PLW3{\left(\frac{P30}{2{\cdot}P21}\right)}^2 .

Examine parameters by typing 'eda' , or select the 'Acqpars' tab and get the 'eda' window by clicking on the ' A ' icon. This view shows the three dimensions, F3(1H), and F2(15N) and F1(T2) in columns. Parameters to check: DS - 32-128 'dummy' scans that are not recorded; allows system to reach steady state equilibration. NS - multiple of 8; increase for increased signal to noise ( S/N increases as √NS ) 3 SW - 1 H spectral width (~12-15 ppm, defined in BioTop) 2 SW - 15 N amide spectral width (~25-40 ppm, defined in BioTop) O1 - 1 H H 2 O offset in Hz (calibrated with BioTop or calibo1p1) O2P - Not used. 13 C offset set internally to the average of CNST21 and CNST22 (for 13 C decoupling with ZGOPTNS -DLABEL_CN ) O3P - 15 N amide offset (~115-120 ppm, defined in bioTop) 3 TD - Number of 1 H time domain real points (~1024-2048, preferably 2 N , keep 3 AQ at ~50-120 ms) 2 TD - Number of 15 N time domain real points (keep 2 AQ at ~30-60 ms) 1 TD - Number of T 2 delay points (should match number of lines in VCLIST ) DIGMOD - 'baseopt' (zero 1st order phase correction in 1 H)
2D F2(15N)-F3(1H) and F1(T2)-F3(1H) planes can be processed with Topspin commands xfb and xf2, respectively.
creator: NAN-KB UGA
